item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good article! Not too long but goes in depth enough to he satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i've updated the article a bit in line with this criticism, and in part to try and reflect the reason for the panther's existence. i'd love if you took another look!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is true. i didn't anticipate an explanation being needed, but it is a loose end. i'll edit this soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks for your in depth criticism, i appreciate that!

you did definitely nail the eyes bit — i was trying to figure out a way to have people drastically "get out" of the hallucination cycle, but i do agree that it's been done before and could be re-done in a better way.

as for the tests, i can see what you mean. i think i wanted to try and explain the vagueness, but maybe that doesn't need to be there. i think my idea was that the foundation should show its entire thought process through the testing, but maybe i can trust my reader more.

thanks for taking the time to write this up! i'll definitely keep this stuff in mind and, should the article not do very well, i will consider rewriting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a fan of the whole "tearing at their eyes and becoming suicidal" aspect of this at all. It cheapens the trauma that would come naturally with seeing your favorite pet torn apart (coming from someone who has such a pet), and is something that usually gets laughed at when included in an article. I feel like there was a subconscious fixation on eyes in this article simply for the body horror, even if it could be only because of the visual hallucination property.

I'm also not a fan of the tests reiterating what was already established in the description. I was also a little confused by the addendum, as they don't actually list what the context for each one is besides a "call log" (which also reiterates the description), an email that should have been included as information in the final test, and a 3rd addenda that could have been a single sentence.

There also doesn't seem to be any deep motive behind killing non-pet owners, when I would think they get off scot-free for not "forcing a pet to domestic life" or something like that. I think death is used too often to attempt to get a scare out of the reader, since essentially anyone exposed to it dies.

I do think that the the tire and remains on it makes for an interesting object for the basis article, and how it was vague in what exactly had died on it— certainly more interesting than a panther. This seems to be in the positives, but I would rewrite it from the ground up.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happened to Agent Brinks? It mentions them being subjected to the hallucinations during recovery, but no mention of their ultimate fate. Feels like a loose end to me.

item.title: big ol' nightmare panther comin' for ya guts
item.content:encoded:
thanks so much to Kakroom and Gabriel Jade for their solid crit.

if this gets deleted, so be it. gotta start somewhere!

made this because this is something that personally scares me and makes me sad (pets dying), which i think is a fairly relatable fear. tried to tie in a lil mystery and a lil big panther boy too.
